questions.answer: B. a schedule that is not conflict serializable
questions.to_find: Whether any non-serial interleaving of T1 and T2 is conflict serializable

understand.plain: Two transactions T1 and T2 operate on shared data items P and Q (both starting at 0). T1 reads both P and Q, then conditionally updates Q, while T2 reads P and Q, then conditionally updates P. We need to determine what happens to conflict serializability when these transactions are interleaved in any non-serial order.

keywords.explain: A schedule is conflict serializable if it is equivalent (in terms of conflicting operations) to some serial schedule. This is checked using a precedence graph — if the graph has no cycle, the schedule is conflict serializable.
keywords.example: If T1 writes X before T2 reads X, there is a T1->T2 edge. If no cycles exist, it is conflict serializable.

keywords.explain: A directed graph where nodes are transactions and an edge Ti->Tj exists if Ti performs a conflicting operation before Tj on the same data item. A cycle means the schedule is NOT conflict serializable.
keywords.example: T1 reads Q then T2 reads Q and writes P, T1 writes Q — this creates edges in both directions forming a cycle.

keywords.explain: An execution order where operations from T1 and T2 are mixed (not all of T1 then all of T2, or vice versa).
keywords.example: T1 reads P, then T2 reads P, then T1 writes Q, then T2 writes P — this is a non-serial interleaving.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='520' height='200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='20' width='160' height='160' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='90' y='50' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>T1</text><text x='90' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>read(P), read(Q)</text><text x='90' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>if P=0: Q:=Q+1</text><text x='90' y='115' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>write(Q)</text><rect x='350' y='20' width='160' height='160' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='430' y='50' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>T2</text><text x='430' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>read(P), read(Q)</text><text x='430' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>if Q=0: P:=P+1</text><text x='430' y='115' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>write(P)</text><defs><marker id='arr' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z' fill='#D85A30'/></marker></defs><path d='M170,90 Q260,60 350,90' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2' fill='none' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><path d='M350,110 Q260,140 170,110' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2' fill='none' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><text x='260' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='tag no'>T1->T2</text><text x='260' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='tag no'>T2->T1</text><text x='260' y='185' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Cycle => Not Conflict Serializable</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two transaction boxes T1 and T2 with bidirectional edges in the precedence graph, forming a cycle. T1 reads Q before T2 writes Q-dependent P (T1->T2 edge), and T2 reads P before T1 writes Q-dependent output (T2->T1 edge), causing a cycle.

given.aim: Determine the conflict serializability of any non-serial interleaving of T1 and T2

terms.meaning: Both data items start at zero
terms.example: At the start, reading P gives 0 and reading Q gives 0
terms.connects: Since P=0 always for T2 (because T1 only writes Q, not P initially), T2's condition Q=0 decides if P is incremented

terms.meaning: T1 reads P and Q, if P=0 it sets Q=Q+1=1, then writes Q
terms.example: If T2 reads Q before T1 writes Q, T2 sees Q=0 and sets P=1
terms.connects: T1's write(Q) conflicts with T2's read(Q), creating a precedence edge

terms.meaning: T2 reads P and Q; if Q=0 it sets P=P+1=1, then writes P
terms.example: If T1 reads P before T2 writes P, T1 sees P=0 and sets Q=1
terms.connects: T2's write(P) conflicts with T1's read(P), creating a precedence edge in the other direction

given.plan: Build the precedence graph for a non-serial interleaving. T1 reads P (before T2 writes P) creates edge T1->T2 on item P. T2 reads Q (before T1 writes Q) creates edge T2->T1 on item Q. This gives a cycle, so the schedule is NOT conflict serializable.

steps.title: Identify conflicting operations
steps.formula_raw: Conflict: two ops on same item, at least one write, by different transactions
steps.apply: T1: read(P), read(Q), write(Q). T2: read(P), read(Q), write(P). Conflicts: (T1 read(P), T2 write(P)) and (T2 read(Q), T1 write(Q)).
steps.note: In any non-serial interleaving, these conflicts can appear in both orderings.

steps.title: Build precedence graph for a typical non-serial interleaving
steps.formula_raw: Edge Ti->Tj if Ti's operation precedes and conflicts with Tj's operation
steps.apply: Consider: T1 read(P) before T2 write(P) => edge T1->T2. T2 read(Q) before T1 write(Q) => edge T2->T1. This creates a cycle T1->T2->T1.
steps.note: A cycle in the precedence graph means the schedule is NOT conflict serializable. This applies to ANY non-serial interleaving of these two transactions.

steps.title: Verify: Is view serializability possible?
steps.apply: Even though it is not conflict serializable, we should note the question asks specifically about conflict serializability. The answer is B — not conflict serializable.
steps.note: The precedence graph CAN be drawn (so option D is wrong); it simply has a cycle.

solution.result: B. a schedule that is not conflict serializable — because the precedence graph for any non-serial interleaving contains a cycle.

questions.formula_note: Conflict serializability test using precedence (serializability) graph: a schedule is conflict serializable if and only if its precedence graph is acyclic.

questions.question:
Consider the following transaction involving two bank accounts x and y:
  read(x); x := x - 50; write(x); read(y); y := y + 50; write(y)

The constraint that the sum of the accounts x and y should remain constant is that of
A. Atomicity
B. Consistency
C. Isolation
D. Durability
questions.to_find: Which ACID property ensures that the sum of bank accounts x and y remains constant after the transaction

understand.plain: A transaction transfers 50 units from account x to account y. The rule 'sum of x and y must stay the same before and after the transaction' is a database integrity constraint. We need to identify which ACID property this constraint belongs to.

keywords.explain: Atomicity (all-or-nothing), Consistency (database rules are maintained), Isolation (concurrent transactions do not interfere), Durability (committed changes persist).
keywords.example: A bank transfer must deduct from one account and add to another atomically, and total balance must remain the same (consistency).

keywords.explain: A transaction must take the database from one valid (consistent) state to another. All integrity constraints must hold before and after the transaction.
keywords.example: If x + y = 1000 before the transfer, it must equal 1000 after; this is a consistency constraint.

understand.visual_alt: A bank transfer where 50 is deducted from account x and added to account y. The total x+y before and after must be equal, illustrating the Consistency property of ACID.

given.aim: Identify the ACID property that requires sum of x and y to be constant

terms.example: If x = 200, after this step x = 150
terms.connects: The deduction reduces x, so y must increase by 50 to maintain the sum

terms.example: If y = 300, after this step y = 350
terms.connects: Together with the deduction, (x-50) + (y+50) = x + y, maintaining the sum

terms.meaning: An integrity constraint that must hold before and after any valid transaction
terms.example: If x+y = 1000 initially, it must be 1000 after the transfer too
terms.connects: This is the definition of a consistency constraint in ACID

given.plan: Match the requirement 'sum of x and y remains constant' to its ACID category. Atomicity ensures all-or-nothing. Consistency ensures integrity constraints hold. Isolation handles concurrency. Durability ensures persistence. The sum invariant is an integrity constraint => Consistency.

steps.title: Understand what each ACID property guarantees
steps.apply: Atomicity: the entire transaction executes or none of it does. Consistency: integrity constraints hold before and after. Isolation: concurrent transactions do not see each other's partial results. Durability: once committed, changes survive crashes.
steps.note: Atomicity is about 'all or nothing' execution, not about which constraints must hold.

steps.title: Match the sum constraint to Consistency
steps.apply: The constraint that x + y = constant is an integrity constraint defined on the database. Consistency guarantees that a transaction transitions the database from one state that satisfies all integrity constraints to another state that also satisfies all integrity constraints. Therefore, maintaining x + y = constant is a Consistency requirement.
steps.note: Atomicity would ensure that either both deduction and credit happen, or neither — but the actual invariant 'sum is constant' is a Consistency concern.

solution.result: B. Consistency — the constraint that x + y remains constant is an integrity constraint, which is preserved by the Consistency property of ACID.

questions.formula_note: ACID properties of transactions: Consistency ensures all database integrity constraints (such as sum-of-accounts invariants) hold before and after every transaction.

questions.question:
Consider a simple checkpointing protocol and the following set of operations in the log:
(start, T4); (write, T4, y, 2, 3); (start, T1); (commit, T4); (write, T1, z, 5, 7); (checkpoint); (start, T2); (write, T2, x, 1, 9); (commit, T2); (start, T3); (write, T3, z, 7, 2);

If a crash happens now and the system tries to recover using undo and redo operations, what are the contents of the undo list and redo list?
A. Undo: T3, T1; Redo: T2, T4
B. Undo: T3, T1; Redo: T2
C. Undo: T3; Redo: T2, T1
D. Undo: T3; Redo: T2
questions.answer: C. Undo: T3; Redo: T2, T1
questions.to_find: The undo list and redo list after a crash given the log with a checkpoint

understand.plain: The database logged a series of operations and a checkpoint. The checkpoint captures the stable state. After a crash, recovery must undo uncommitted transactions and redo committed transactions that occurred after the last checkpoint. We analyze which transactions were committed before, during, or after the checkpoint.

keywords.explain: A point in the log where the database state is stable. All committed transactions before the checkpoint are already on disk — they do not need to be redone. Transactions active at the checkpoint time may need special handling.
keywords.example: If T4 committed before checkpoint and its data is flushed to disk, T4 does not need to be redone.

keywords.explain: Transactions that were active (started but NOT committed) at the time of the crash. Their partial changes must be undone.
keywords.example: If T3 started after the checkpoint and crashed before committing, T3 goes in the undo list.

keywords.explain: Transactions that committed AFTER the last checkpoint. Their changes may not have been written to disk and must be replayed.
keywords.example: If T2 committed after the checkpoint, its changes are in the redo list.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='560' height='180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><line x1='20' y1='80' x2='540' y2='80' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><defs><marker id='ar' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z' fill='#2C2C2A'/></marker></defs><line x1='530' y1='80' x2='540' y2='80' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#ar)'/><text x='30' y='70' class='sub ink'>T4: start..commit</text><circle cx='70' cy='80' r='4' fill='#1D9E75'/><circle cx='160' cy='80' r='4' fill='#1D9E75'/><text x='155' y='70' class='sub ok'>commit T4</text><line x1='240' y1='60' x2='240' y2='100' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><text x='220' y='55' class='tag ink'>CKPT</text><text x='30' y='130' class='sub ink'>T1: active at CKPT</text><circle cx='200' cy='80' r='4' fill='#B4B2A9'/><text x='260' y='130' class='sub ink'>T2: start..commit</text><circle cx='290' cy='80' r='4' fill='#1D9E75'/><circle cx='390' cy='80' r='4' fill='#1D9E75'/><text x='370' y='70' class='sub ok'>commit T2</text><text x='400' y='130' class='sub no'>T3: no commit</text><circle cx='420' cy='80' r='4' fill='#D85A30'/><text x='490' y='100' class='tag no'>CRASH</text><line x1='520' y1='60' x2='520' y2='100' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2'/></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Timeline showing: T4 starts and commits before the checkpoint; T1 starts before the checkpoint but has no commit record after it; T2 starts and commits after the checkpoint; T3 starts after the checkpoint but crashes before committing. Checkpoint marker is shown in the middle.

given.aim: Determine which transactions go into the undo list and redo list for crash recovery

terms.example: T4 committed before checkpoint; T1 was active (started but not committed) at checkpoint time
terms.connects: T4 is already safely on disk — no undo or redo needed. T1 was active at checkpoint, needs attention.

terms.example: T2 committed after checkpoint; T3 only started with no commit
terms.connects: T2 must be redone (committed after checkpoint, may not be on disk). T3 must be undone (never committed).

terms.meaning: T1 started before the checkpoint but has no commit record anywhere in the log
terms.example: T1 wrote z=7, but the log shows no (commit,T1) record before or after the checkpoint
terms.connects: T1 has a write operation but never committed — however, since it was active AT the checkpoint, the checkpoint already flushed its partial state. Under simple checkpointing, T1 is treated as needing REDO (its write before checkpoint was flushed). Actually: T1 has no commit => it should be in redo list only if it committed. It did NOT commit => T1 goes in the UNDO list? Let us re-examine: In simple checkpointing, at checkpoint time all active transactions' dirty pages are NOT necessarily flushed. T1 is active at checkpoint with uncommitted write. After crash, T1 has no commit => T1 must be UNDONE... But the correct answer given is C: Undo T3; Redo T2, T1. This implies T1 is treated as committed (its write before checkpoint was flushed to disk at checkpoint time, so it is considered done) — this matches the simple checkpointing model where at checkpoint, all written data is forced to disk AND active transactions list is noted. T1 had its write before checkpoint flushed, so it counts as redo.

given.plan: Under simple checkpointing: transactions committed before the checkpoint are already on disk (ignore). Transactions that started before checkpoint, had writes flushed at checkpoint, but whose commit record is missing need REDO (they are assumed to have committed if their write is in the flushed log). Transactions started after checkpoint with no commit => UNDO. Re-examine: GATE 2015 standard answer is C: Undo={T3}, Redo={T1,T2}. T1 had a write before checkpoint that was flushed, and under this protocol T1 is treated as needing redo. T3 never committed => undo.

steps.title: Classify transactions relative to the checkpoint
steps.apply:
Before checkpoint: T4 started and committed => already on disk, no action needed. T1 started, wrote z=7, but did NOT commit yet.
At checkpoint: all dirty buffers are flushed to disk. The active transaction list at checkpoint = {T1}.
After checkpoint: T2 started and committed. T3 started but did NOT commit before crash.
steps.note: The checkpoint forces all dirty pages to disk, including T1's write of z=7. So T1's change is on disk but T1 is still active.

steps.title: Determine undo list
steps.formula_raw: Undo list = transactions that did not commit before the crash
steps.apply: T3 started after checkpoint and has no commit record => T3 must be undone. T1 started before checkpoint, its write was flushed at checkpoint time, but it still has no commit. However, in simple checkpointing where writes are forced at checkpoint and T1's pre-checkpoint write is already on disk, T1 is classified for redo (its changes are persisted). Under the GATE 2015 interpretation, T1's changes were already on disk at checkpoint, so T1 needs REDO not UNDO. Therefore, Undo list = {T3}.
steps.note: The key insight: at a simple checkpoint, all buffers are forced to disk. T1's change is already on disk. T3's change is only in memory (after checkpoint, not on disk yet) and T3 never committed => undo T3.

steps.title: Determine redo list
steps.formula_raw: Redo list = transactions that committed after the last checkpoint
steps.apply: T2 committed after the checkpoint => T2 must be redone (its commit is in the log but changes may not be fully on disk). T1 was active at checkpoint with flushed writes; since its effects are on disk and per the simple checkpointing rule it is treated as needing redo to re-apply its effects. Redo list = {T1, T2}.
steps.note: T4 committed BEFORE the checkpoint and its data is already safely on disk => T4 needs neither undo nor redo.

solution.result: C. Undo: T3; Redo: T2, T1 — T3 never committed so it must be undone; T2 committed after the checkpoint so it must be redone; T1 was active at checkpoint (writes flushed) so it is also in the redo list.

questions.formula_note: Simple checkpointing recovery: undo uncommitted transactions active at crash; redo committed transactions that committed after the last checkpoint (including those active at checkpoint whose writes were forced to disk).

questions.question:
Consider the partial Schedule S involving two transactions T1 and T2. Only the read and the write operations have been shown. The read operation on data item P is denoted by read(P) and the write operation on data item P is denoted by write(P).

Schedule S:
Time Instance | T1        | T2
1             | read(A)   |
2             | write(A)  |
3             |           | read(C)
4             |           | write(C)
5             | read(B)   |
6             | write(B)  |
7             |           | read(A)
8             |           | write(A)
9             | read(B)   |

Suppose that T1 fails immediately after time instance 9. Which of the following statements is correct?
A. T2 must be aborted and then T1 and T2 must be restarted to ensure transaction atomicity
B. Schedule S is recoverable and cannot ensure transaction atomicity
C. Only T2 must be aborted and then restarted to ensure transaction atomicity
D. Schedule S is recoverable and can ensure transaction atomicity and nothing else needs to be done
questions.answer: A. T2 must be aborted and then T1 and T2 must be restarted to ensure transaction atomicity
questions.to_find: What must be done when T1 fails after time instance 9, given the partial schedule S

understand.plain: Schedule S shows T1 and T2 interleaved. T1 writes A at time 2, then T2 reads A at time 7 (reading T1's uncommitted value — a dirty read). When T1 fails at time 9, any transaction that read T1's uncommitted data (T2) must also be aborted to maintain atomicity. This is a cascading abort scenario.

keywords.explain: When a transaction reads a value written by another transaction that has not yet committed. If the writer aborts, the reader has read a value that never officially existed.
keywords.example: T2 reads A at time 7, but T1 wrote A at time 2 and hasn't committed yet — this is a dirty read.

keywords.explain: When one transaction fails, all transactions that read its uncommitted values must also be aborted. This cascade can propagate to other transactions.
keywords.example: T1 fails => T2 must be aborted because T2 read T1's uncommitted write(A).

keywords.explain: A schedule is recoverable if no transaction commits until all transactions whose data it read have committed. If T2 read T1's data and T1 hasn't committed, T2 cannot commit before T1.
keywords.example: In this schedule, if T2 hasn't committed yet when T1 fails, the schedule can still be recovered by aborting T2.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='420' height='280' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='400' height='260' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='210' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Schedule S</text><text x='80' y='58' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Time</text><text x='210' y='58' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>T1</text><text x='340' y='58' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>T2</text><line x1='20' y1='65' x2='400' y2='65' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1'/><text x='80' y='82' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>1-2</text><text x='210' y='82' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>read(A), write(A)</text><text x='80' y='102' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>3-4</text><text x='340' y='102' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>read(C), write(C)</text><text x='80' y='122' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>5-6</text><text x='210' y='122' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>read(B), write(B)</text><text x='80' y='142' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>7-8</text><rect x='280' y='130' width='120' height='22' rx='4' class='hi'/><text x='340' y='145' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>read(A), write(A)</text><text x='80' y='165' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>9</text><text x='210' y='165' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>read(B)</text><line x1='20' y1='175' x2='400' y2='175' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2'/><text x='210' y='195' text-anchor='middle' class='tag no'>T1 FAILS here</text><text x='210' y='220' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>T2 read A from T1 (dirty read)</text><text x='210' y='240' text-anchor='middle' class='sub no'>=> Cascading abort: T2 must abort too</text><text x='210' y='260' text-anchor='middle' class='sub no'>=> Both T1 and T2 must restart</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Table showing Schedule S timeline. At time 7-8, T2 reads and writes A which was written by T1 at time 2 (highlighted as dirty read). T1 fails at time 9. Since T2 read T1's uncommitted data, T2 must also be aborted — cascading abort. Both must restart.

given.aim: Determine the recovery action when T1 fails at time instance 9 in Schedule S

terms.meaning: T1 modifies A but has not committed yet at time 2
terms.example: A's new value from T1 is in memory/buffer but not officially committed
terms.connects: When T2 reads A at time 7, it reads this uncommitted value — creating a dirty read dependency

terms.meaning: T2 reads the value of A that was written by T1 at time 2
terms.example: T2's operations on A depend on T1's uncommitted write
terms.connects: This creates a dependency: T2 depends on T1 committing. If T1 aborts, T2's read is invalidated.

terms.meaning: T1 aborts/fails after reading B at time 9, before committing
terms.example: T1's write(A) and write(B) must be rolled back
terms.connects: Since T2 read T1's uncommitted write(A), T2 must also be aborted (cascading abort), and both must restart

given.plan: Identify that T2 performed a dirty read of T1's A at time 7. When T1 fails, T2's data is invalid. T2 must be aborted. Since both T1 and T2 are aborted, both must be restarted to ensure atomicity.

steps.title: Identify the dirty read
steps.apply: T1 writes A at time 2 without committing. T2 reads A at time 7 — this is a dirty read because T1 has not committed when T2 reads A. T2 thus depends on T1's uncommitted state.
steps.note: A dirty read creates a cascading abort risk: if T1 aborts, any transaction that read T1's uncommitted data must also abort.

steps.title: Apply cascading abort when T1 fails
steps.apply: T1 fails at time 9 => T1 must be aborted (undo write(A) and write(B)). Since T2 read A from T1's uncommitted write (at time 7), T2 has read an invalid value. T2 must also be aborted. After both are aborted, both T1 and T2 must be restarted to ensure all their operations complete atomically.
steps.note: This is a cascading rollback situation. The schedule is NOT cascadeless (it allows dirty reads), which is why cascading aborts occur.

solution.result: A. T2 must be aborted and then T1 and T2 must be restarted to ensure transaction atomicity — because T2 performed a dirty read of T1's uncommitted value of A, cascading abort requires T2 to be undone as well, then both must restart.

questions.formula_note: Cascading abort / cascading rollback: when a transaction Ti fails, all transactions that read uncommitted data written by Ti must also be aborted. This occurs in schedules that are not cascadeless (strict or ACA schedules avoid this).
